question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, at what sintering temperature was ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO) densified, and what was the thickness of the outer oxidized layer formed after oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour?
reference_answer: ZSLO was sintered at 1850°C and attained above 99% theoretical density in less than 5 minutes. After oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour, the outer oxidized layer was greater than 250 micrometers thick and predominantly composed of a continuous bright phase with isolated dark regions.
evidence_any: ZSLO sintered at 1850 ◦C, ZSLB sintered at 1750 ◦C, and ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦C, attained above 99%
The outer >250 ␮m thick layer (1) is predominantly a continuous bright phase
excerpts: All samples, ZSLO sintered at 1850 ◦C, ZSLB sintered at 1750 ◦C, and ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦C, attained above 99% of theoretical density in less than 5 min. Phase analysis confirmed that all starting materials remained unchanged after SPS and did not show any secondary crystalline phase analysed by XRD. Fig. 1 shows typical bright-field TEM images of (a) ZSLO, (b) ZSLB and (c) ZSGO. Fig. 1(a) shows the distribution of SiC and ZrB2 grains in ZSLO. No grain boundary phases were observed. Previous study suggested that during liquid phase sintering of SiC, La2O3 reacts with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a lanthanum silicate phase. Although we have no direct evidence for this in the areas analysed, it seems likely that at the high sintering temperature, ∼1800 ◦C, used in the present study at least some of the RE oxide would have reacted with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a liquid grain boundary silicate (RE2Si2O7) phase.

Fig. 2(a) is an electron image (EI) of cross-section surface microstructures of oxidised ZSLO at 1600 ◦C taken in FIB station. Fig. 2(a) reveals three oxidised layers. The outer >250 ␮m thick layer (1) is predominantly a continuous bright phase with isolated (5–30 ␮m diameter) dark regions present. The intermediate layers above the unaffected bulk material are about 50 ␮m thick. Two intermediate layers can be distinctly seen in this FIB electron image. SIMS data collected (not shown here) on the dark contrast phase (layer 2) in Fig. 2 (b) consists mainly of Si and O whereas the bright phase (layer 3) is predominantly Zr and O. SIMS analysis was carried out at the interface between the top surface layer and the top intermediate layer in Fig. 2(a).

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what oxidation temperature and duration were used for processing ZSLO (ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3), and what was a notable finding regarding boron retention in the oxidized layer?
reference_answer: Oxidation studies were performed for 1 hour at 1600°C in an open hearth furnace with heating at 10°C/min. A notable finding was that boron was detected in the oxidized top surface even after 1 hour oxidation at 1600°C, indicating that not all boria was removed and the volatility of B2O3 in borosilicate liquid is relatively low.
answer_keys: not all B2O3 could have escaped
evidence_any: heating samples to 1600 ◦C at a rate of 10 ◦C/min, holding for 1 h and finally air-quenching
even after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C, boron was not completely removed
excerpts: Oxidation studies were performed for the sintered (99% dense) samples in a bottom loaded open hearth MoSi2 furnace by heating samples to 1600 ◦C at a rate of 10 ◦C/min, holding for 1 h and finally air-quenching to room temperature by lowering the specimen chamber. Oxidised samples were cut, mounted in epoxy resin moulds and polished down to 1 ␮m surface finish. Microstructures of the sintered and oxidised samples were observed under a scanning electron microscope (SEM) fitted with a field-emission gun (FEG, model LEO15) and energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS, X-Max 20, Oxford instruments; Abingdon, UK) elemental analysis was performed to assist phase identification.

SIMS analysis revealed the presence of Si, Zr, La, B and their oxides in the oxidised top surface. Interestingly, boron was also detected indicating that in ZSLO, even after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C, boron was not completely removed. Reports on the detection of boron in the oxidised layers using EDS in the SEM are sparse with limited ability to detect boron. Since the volatility of B2O3 in borosilicate [BS] liquid is low, not all B2O3 could have escaped from the melt. In positive mass spectra, the 27.8amu atomic mass reflects 11B-16O with intensities that reflect the boron isotope natural abundance.

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what material composition and oxidation conditions were applied to ZSLB (ZS20/10 wt.% LaB6), and what was the measured thickness of the outer oxidized layer?
reference_answer: ZSLB was sintered at 1750°C and oxidized at 1600°C for 1 hour. After oxidation, ZSLB exhibited a similar outer oxidized layer thickness of approximately 250 micrometers as ZSLO, with the outer layer approximately 120 micrometers thick and dense, while the intermediate layer was approximately 50 micrometers thick and porous.
evidence_any: ZSLB had similar oxidation layer thickness (∼250 ␮m) as observed in ZSLO after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C
The outer layer is ∼120 ␮m thick and dense while the intermediate layer is ∼50 ␮m thick and porous
excerpts: UHTCs namely, ZS/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO), ZS/10 wt.% LaB6 (ZSLB) and ZS/10 wt.% Gd2O3 (ZSGO) were densified in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace (FCT Systems, Germany) using the sintering parameters given in Table 1. Oxidation studies were performed for the sintered (99% dense) samples in a bottom loaded open hearth MoSi2 furnace by heating samples to 1600 ◦C at a rate of 10 ◦C/min, holding for 1 h and finally air-quenching to room temperature by lowering the specimen chamber. Oxidised samples were cut, mounted in epoxy resin moulds and polished down to 1 ␮m surface finish.

ZSLB had similar oxidation layer thickness (∼250 ␮m) as observed in ZSLO after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C. Microstructure and SIMS analyses from different regions in the cross-section of the oxidised surface are shown in Fig. 7. The oxidised surface is heterogeneous and contains a variety of phase morphologies. In some regions, it appears to be compact with grain boundary phases presumably derived from liquid. Two layers can be seen at low magnification in Fig. 7(a). The outer layer is ∼120 ␮m thick and dense while the intermediate layer is ∼50 ␮m thick and porous. The porosity present in the intermediate layer may reduce strength degradation after oxidation.

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what sintering temperature was used for ZSGO (ZS20/10 wt.% Gd2O3), and what was a distinguishing characteristic of its oxidized surface compared to baseline SiC–ZrB2?
reference_answer: ZSGO was sintered at 1800°C. A distinguishing characteristic was that after oxidation for 1 hour at 1600°C, ZSGO had its top surface covered mostly by crystalline oxides in contrast to the amorphous silicate coating formed in baseline SiC–ZrB2 UHTCs.
answer_keys: contrast to the amorphous silicate coating
evidence_any: ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦C, attained above 99% of theoretical density in less than 5 min
ZSGO after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C had its top surface covered mostly by crystalline oxides
excerpts: All samples, ZSLO sintered at 1850 ◦C, ZSLB sintered at 1750 ◦C, and ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦C, attained above 99% of theoretical density in less than 5 min. Phase analysis confirmed that all starting materials remained unchanged after SPS and did not show any secondary crystalline phase analysed by XRD. Fig. 1 shows typical bright-field TEM images of (a) ZSLO, (b) ZSLB and (c) ZSGO. Fig. 1(c) shows the bright-field TEM image of ZSGO whereby SiC grain is surrounded by Gd2O3 grains. In addition to the ZrB2, SiC, and Gd2O3 starting materials, other phases present are ZrO2 (darkest contrast) and gadolinium silicate (likely formed by reaction of silica on the SiC with the Gd2O3 on sintering and whose morphology suggests it is glass).

3.2.3. Oxidation of ZS20/10 wt.% Gd2O3 (ZSGO). ZSGO after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C had its top surface covered mostly by crystalline oxides in contrast to the amorphous silicate coating formed in base line SiC–ZrB2 UHTCs. Fig. 11(a) shows a BS electron image of cross-section showing different layers with irregular top layer thickness. Fig. 11(b) shows a bright-field TEM image of the oxidised layer of ZSGO. Two different contrast phases predominate: a dark grey phase on the right side and light grey phase on the left side. Regions near the interface of two grains are observed at higher magnification and analysed in Fig. 11(c).

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what common oxidized layer structure features were observed across all three rare-earth-doped ZrB2/SiC samples after 1 hour oxidation at 1600°C?
reference_answer: All three samples—ZSLO, ZSLB, and ZSGO—exhibited a protective top crystalline oxide layer, intermediate layer(s) with borosilicate glass containing immiscible liquid, and unoxidized bulk material beneath. These observations were used to design oxidation and thermal protection systems for UHTCs.
answer_keys: intermediate layer(s) with BS containing immiscible liquid
design a candidate oxidation and thermal protection system
evidence_any: a protective top crystalline oxide layer, intermediate layer(s) with BS containing immiscible liquid and unoxidised bulk material
used to design a candidate oxidation and thermal protection system using UHTCs
excerpts: In all three samples (ZSLO ([Fig. 2](a)), ZSLB ([Fig. 7](a)) and ZSGO ([Fig. 11](a)))) after oxidation for 1h at 1600 ◦C d, the common features observed after oxidation at 1600 ◦C from their cross-section microstructures are a protective top crystalline oxide layer, intermediate layer(s) with BS containing immiscible liquid and unoxidised bulk material. These observations have been used to design a candidate oxidation and thermal protection system using UHTCs in which reduced strength degradation after oxidation is expected. Although, ZrB2–SiC oxidised at 1600 ◦C had much thinner and more compact coatings, due to the fact it is a glassy phase, at higher velocity for a long time it becomes soft and will be blown off subsequently.

Detailed structural and chemical analyses of the oxide layers in rare earth containing ZrB2-based UHTCs after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦C revealed that rare earth additions aid formation of crystalline high melting refractory oxide(s) such as lanthanum zirconate which, along with ZrO2 and silicate phases were observed on dense, thick outer oxidised surface layers. The layer-by-layer analysis across the cross-section of oxidised samples revealed that boron remained even on the top (outer) surface. However, the amount of boron present in the deeper intermediate layers is higher most likely due to boria evaporation from the surface.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what was the determined formation temperature for ZrC based on thermal analysis, and what porosity was achieved in the final porous ZrC ceramic?
reference_answer: Based on thermal analysis, the formation temperature (reaction-complete temperature) for ZrC was determined to be 1620°C, with the reaction starting at 1260°C. The final porous ZrC ceramic achieved a high porosity of 68.74% after partial sintering at 1850°C.
evidence_any: reaction-starting temperature is 1260 ◦C and the reaction-complete temperature is 1620 ◦C
exhibit homogeneous porous microstructure with grain sizes in the range of 300–600 nm and 200–500 nm, high porosity of 68.74%
excerpts: In order to determine the synthesis temperature of ZrC and HfC and control the gas releasing process, the reactant powders were heated to 1750 ◦C in a thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA 409 CD/7/403/5/G, Germany) in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦C/min. Meanwhile, shrinkage behavior was monitored using a dilatometer (NETZSCH DIL 402 E, Germany) through heating the columnar shaped green bodies (7 mm in diameter and 15 mm in height) up to 1850 ◦C in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦C/min to investigate the shrinkage behavior during reaction and sintering. The linear shrinkage (dL/L0) and linear shrinkage rate curves together with the corresponding thermogravimetry (TG) and differential thermogravimetry (DTG) curves are shown in Fig. 2.

From Fig. 2(a), an obvious mass loss is occurred in the range of 1260–1620 ◦C on the TG curve of ZrO2/C, suggesting that the reaction-starting temperature is 1260 ◦C and the reaction-complete temperature is 1620 ◦C. This mass loss is resulted from reaction (6) due to the releasing of CO. Meanwhile, linear shrinkage of up to 18.1% is observed. Intriguingly, the shape of TG curve is consistent with that of linear shrinkage curve, which indicates that the reduced reaction (6) is accompanied by mass loss and shrinkage. Above 1620 ◦C, the reduction reaction is completed and the shrinkage rate becomes slow, indicating that the shrinkage is resulted from partial sintering between ZrC grains.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what formation temperature was selected for HfC synthesis based on thermal analysis, and what room-temperature thermal conductivity was achieved for the resulting porous HfC?
reference_answer: The formation temperature for HfC was selected as 1580°C based on thermal analysis showing that the reduction reaction starts at 1400°C and completes at 1710°C. The resulting porous HfC ceramic achieved a very low room-temperature thermal conductivity of 1.01 W·m−1 K−1.
answer_keys: formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦C and 1580 ◦C
low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.01
evidence_any: formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦C and 1580 ◦C, respectively, by heating at a rate of 5 ◦C/min

The as-prepared porous ZrC and HfC exhibit homogeneous porous microstructure with grain sizes in the range of 300–600 nm and 200–500 nm, high porosity of 68.74% and 77.82%, low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 and 1.01 W·m−1 K−1
excerpts: According to the thermal analysis results, formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦C and 1580 ◦C, respectively, by heating at a rate of 5 ◦C/min and the partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦C. Porous ZrC and HfC were prepared through gas-released in-situ reactions followed by high-temperature sintering, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The reactant powders were uniaxial pressed into columnar shaped green bodies of 30 mm in diameter. Similarly, the reduction reaction of HfO2/C to form HfC starts at 1400 ◦C and completes at 1710 ◦C. The maximum gas-releasing rate and shrinkage rate temperature are 1610 ◦C and 1635 ◦C, respectively.

The as-prepared porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs possess excellent shape stability without formation of cracks and other defects, as shown in insert of Fig. 4. The diameters of as-prepared porous ZrC and HfC samples are 23.41 mm and 23.91 mm, respectively. The sintered density, porosity, radical shrinkage and compressive strength of ZrC and HfC UHTCs are listed in Table 1. XRD patterns of the crushed samples in Fig. 4 show that the peaks coincide with rock-salt structured ZrC and HfC, respectively, indicating that the porous UHTCs prepared by in-situ synthesis/high-temperature partial sintering are ZrC and HfC respectively. The room temperature thermal conductivities are 1.12 and 1.01 W·m−1 K−1, respectively, compared to 20.5 and 20.0 W·m−1 K−1 for dense ZrC and HfC.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what molar ratio of reactant powders was used in the preparation of ZrO2/C and HfO2/C green bodies, and what grain size ranges were achieved in the resulting porous ceramics?
reference_answer: Two kinds of reactant powders with molar ratios of ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 were ball-milled for 20 hours. The resulting porous ZrC and HfC ceramics achieved grain size ranges of 300–600 nm and 200–500 nm respectively, forming homogeneous porous microstructures.
evidence_any: two kinds of reactant powders with the molar ratios of ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 were ball-milled in absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 h
The grain size of porous ZrC and HfC samples is in the range of 300–600 nm and 200–500 nm
excerpts: The raw materials used in the present study were ZrO2 and HfO2 powders (99.9%, 1 ␮m, China New Metal Materials Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China) and carbon black (99%, -100 mesh, Mudanjiang Qianjin Reagent Co., Ltd, Mudanjiang, China). According to Eqs. (6) and (7), two kinds of reactant powders with the molar ratios of ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 were ball-milled in absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 h using polyurethane jar and zirconia grinding balls. After ball-milling, the slurries were dried in an oven until the alcohol evaporated completely. Then the powders were sieved through a 120-mesh screen to obtain homogeneous reactants.

Microstructure of fracture surface shows that the as-prepared UHTCs possess homogeneous porous structure formed by partial sintering. According to the matter transport by surface diffusion or evaporation-condensation process, the porous structure is formed during high-temperature sintering process. Moreover, the formation and escape of CO gas can prevent the densification of porous UHTCs and thereby generates lots of interconnected pores in final samples with high porosity. The grain size of porous ZrC and HfC samples is in the range of 300–600 nm (Fig. 5(a)) and 200–500 nm (Fig. 5(c)), and the pore size for both porous ZrC and HfC is in the range of 1.0–1.5 ␮m.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what partial sintering temperature was applied after the in-situ reaction stage, and how did the thermal conductivity of the resulting porous ZrC compare to dense ZrC?
reference_answer: The partial sintering temperature was fixed at 1850°C for both ZrC and HfC after the in-situ reaction stage. The resulting porous ZrC achieved a room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 W·m−1 K−1, which is nearly 1/20 of the thermal conductivity of dense ZrC (20.5 W·m−1 K−1).
answer_keys: partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦C
nearly 1/20 of those for dense materials
evidence_any: the partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦C
The room temperature thermal conductivities are 1.12 and 1.01 W·m−1 K−1, respectively, compared to 20.5 and 20.0 W·m−1 K−1 for dense ZrC and HfC, which is nearly 1/20 of those for dense materials

excerpts: According to the thermal analysis results, formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦C and 1580 ◦C, respectively, by heating at a rate of 5 ◦C/min and the partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦C. Porous ZrC and HfC were prepared through gas-released in-situ reactions followed by high-temperature sintering, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The reactant powders were uniaxial pressed into columnar shaped green bodies of 30 mm in diameter. Based on these results, for preparing porous HfC, the green bodies are heated to 1580 ◦C and then to 1850 ◦C for 1 h at a heating rate of 5 ◦C/min. Selecting the same partial sintering temperature and dwell time for both ZrC and HfC will result in different degree of sintering, grain size and strength.

Achieving low thermal conductivity is the main goal of this work. Fig. 7 shows the thermal conductivity and thermal diffusivity of porous ZrC and HfC. It can be seen that porous ZrC and HfC prepared by the above method exhibit low thermal conductivities in the temperature range of 25–900 ◦C. And the thermal conductivity did not change significantly with the increase of temperature. The slight increasing-decreasing-increasing is mainly due to combined effects of electronic (e) and lattice (L) contributions. The room temperature thermal conductivities are 1.12 and 1.01 W·m−1 K−1, respectively, compared to 20.5 and 20.0 W·m−1 K−1 for dense ZrC and HfC, which is nearly 1/20 of those for dense materials due to the small grain size and high porosity.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what temperature range showed obvious mass loss in the thermal analysis of ZrO2/C, and how did the thermal gravimetry curve relate to the shrinkage behavior observed?
reference_answer: Thermal analysis of ZrO2/C showed obvious mass loss occurring in the range of 1260–1620°C, resulting from the carbothermal reduction reaction releasing CO. Notably, the shape of the TG curve was consistent with that of the linear shrinkage curve, indicating that the reduction reaction was accompanied by both mass loss and shrinkage due to CO gas release.
answer_keys: mass loss is occurred in the range of 1260–1620 ◦C
TG curve is consistent with that of linear shrinkage curve
reduction reaction is accompanied by mass loss and shrinkage
evidence_any: From Fig. 2(a), an obvious mass loss is occurred in the range of 1260–1620 ◦C on the TG curve of ZrO2/C, suggesting that the reaction-starting temperature is 1260 ◦C and the reaction-complete temperature is 1620 ◦C. This mass loss is resulted from reaction (6) due to the releasing of CO
Intriguingly, the shape of TG curve is consistent with that of linear shrinkage curve, which indicates that the reduced reaction (6) is accompanied by mass loss and shrinkage
excerpts: The linear shrinkage (dL/L0) and linear shrinkage rate (defined as (1/L0)dL/dt, with L0 being the height of columnar shaped green bodies at room temperature) curves together with the corresponding thermogravimetry (TG) and differential thermogravimetry (DTG) curves are shown in Fig. 2, which can visually reflect the mass loss and shrinkage of samples during the heating process. It can be seen that the shrinkage curves are divided into two parts by apparent rate change in the range of 1200–1850 ◦C.

From Fig. 2(a), an obvious mass loss is occurred in the range of 1260–1620 ◦C on the TG curve of ZrO2/C, suggesting that the reaction-starting temperature is 1260 ◦C and the reaction-complete temperature is 1620 ◦C. This mass loss is resulted from reaction (6) due to the releasing of CO. Meanwhile, linear shrinkage of up to 18.1% is observed. Intriguingly, the shape of TG curve is consistent with that of linear shrinkage curve, which indicates that the reduced reaction (6) is accompanied by mass loss and shrinkage. Above 1620 ◦C, the reduction reaction is completed and the shrinkage rate becomes slow, indicating that the shrinkage is resulted from partial sintering between ZrC grains.
